claim_chain: LAI-PrEP is highly effective but requires appropriate testing strategies
Different testing approaches (RDT, NAT, immunoassay) have trade-offs in accuracy versus accessibility
RDTs provide similar clinical outcomes to NAT with faster results and lower costs
NAT offers higher sensitivity but minimal population-level clinical benefit
RDT-based strategies are more scalable and accessible for resource-limited settings
Testing requirements should prioritize flexibility to avoid barriers to access
WHO guidance supporting RDTs enables simpler and more user-centred approaches

typical_pattern: Provide supporting evidence from meta-analysis or included studies
Compare alternative approaches (RDT vs NAT vs immunoassay)
Connect to broader policy or public health context

likely_priorities: Diagnostic accuracy and sensitivity differences between testing strategies
Clinical significance of delayed HIV detection
Development and prevention of drug resistance mutations
Generalizability across different settings and populations
likely_attacks: Selection bias in assessment of performance accuracy
Lack of prospective evidence for NAT preventing resistance development
Limited evidence on HIV self-testing for LAI-PrEP
Insufficient data on downstream economic effects of missed diagnoses
Reliance on retrospective NAT use rather than real-time clinical decision-making
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly acknowledges 'The overall certainty of evidence was low' and 'We noted that our assessment of performance accuracy in different testing strategies may introduce selection bias.' It addresses concerns about resistance by stating 'there is no direct evidence that NAT-based testing would prevent the development of resistance' and 'there is no prospective evidence that earlier NAT detection would have prevented the development of INSTI RAMs.' The paper notes 'There was limited evidence of HIV self-testing for LAI-PrEP delivery' and 'the included studies did not provide sufficient prospective data to quantify these downstream effects' regarding economic implications. It also emphasizes that 'NAT was used retrospectively on stored samples in HPTN 083 and 084—not prospectively as part of real-time clinical decision-making.'

examples.quoted_phrase: our analysis suggests this has minimal impact on absolute clinical outcomes at the population level
RDTs provide several advantages for patients' outcomes and management, including quicker results, faster linkage to care and ART initiation, lower costs, and higher feasibility and accessibility
the transmission risk associated with these infections is likely to be lower than for PrEP-naive individuals
This suggests that a lot of resources are needed for minimal gain, using a public health lens
there is no direct evidence that NAT-based testing would prevent the development of resistance

phrase_evidence.phrase: This systematic review synthesises evidence on different LAI-PrEP testing strategies
A key contribution of this work is its focus on the clinical utility and program delivery implications
We found similarities in outcomes between RDT, laboratory-based immunoassay and NAT-based testing strategies
While NAT offers benefits in higher diagnostic sensitivity
RDTs provide several advantages for patients' outcomes and management
Further evidence on implementing HIVST to enhance access to LAI-PrEP is needed
Integrating RDT-based testing into LAI-PrEP programs offers some advantages over NAT and laboratory-based immunoassays
To ensure effective and equitable PrEP access, HIV testing must be feasible, accessible, and scalable
Our findings showed that RDT provided quicker results than NAT
This difference could be from operational and communication delays rather than laboratory processing time
Simplified and affordable testing approaches that rely on RDTs and HIVST are important to ensuring LAI-PrEP has a public health impact
Our findings highlight the benefits of transitioning global programming to RDTs
guidance that requires confirmatory laboratory-based testing prior to, or for continuing, LAI-PrEP initiation warrants careful consideration
making it a mandatory prerequisite may have unintended consequences
the included studies did not provide sufficient prospective data to quantify these downstream effects
A modelling study shows that NAT adds significant cost to CAB-LA rollout programs in LMICs, but provides a small benefit
This does not imply that NAT has no role in LAI-PrEP delivery
our analysis suggests this has minimal impact on absolute clinical outcomes at the population level
the transmission risk associated with these infections is likely to be lower than for PrEP-naive individuals
there is no prospective evidence that earlier NAT detection would have prevented the development of INSTI RAMs
the benefit of a slightly earlier diagnosis may be limited
This suggests that a lot of resources are needed for minimal gain, using a public health lens
it is important to note the limitations of these data
there is no direct evidence that NAT-based testing would prevent the development of resistance
RDT-based testing strategies have comparable accuracy to laboratory-based strategies and are more accessible and scalable
adoption of new WHO guidance supporting the use of RDTs can enable simpler, more affordable, and user-centred HIV testing approaches

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, author names, or invented numbers were included. The profile is based entirely on the provided abstract, discussion, and figure legends.

_phrase_violations.phrase: RDTs provide several advantages for patients' outcomes and management
RDTs provide several advantages for patients' outcomes and management, including quicker results, faster linkage to care and ART initiation, lower costs, and higher feasibility and accessibility
